title: Etherfields - More Thoughts (spoiler warnings given)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=a6qWlOZtG3E

Daniel (@dungeondive) returns to Etherfields to field six questions from patrons and the wider community. On replay value, he makes his experiential case — a great campaign is like a fine scotch or a ribeye: worth it even once, and there's some replay here regardless. On minis and add-ons, he's characteristically blunt: skip them; he'd rather have art-matched standees. On trial-and-error and the death spiral, both are real but manageable — solo, he simply takes a dream's negative result, then replays it for the positive rather than grinding. There is a cohesive overarching narrative plus per-character stories you piece together. And no, he never did cut the board — it warped in the garage and he doesn't care.

The bulk is question six, a spoiler-rich defence against "the slumbers are repetitive / the loop is too grindy." He walks through the layered goals stacked onto the overworld circuit: chasing the mystery path, removing flaw cards, harvesting experience via the winter card, the tantalising blue door, advancing progress cards, routing your circuit around the Delta-phase reset, plus encounter set-pieces — a dog-taming dexterity minigame, the nightmare janitor spatial puzzle, a travelling merchant. New things arrive at a steady clip; familiar faces feel like a neighbourhood. He's having a fantastic time.

----

When a game leans on a repeating core loop, what keeps it fresh for you — new content drip-fed in, or layered goals that change *why* you're doing the same actions?
